# WhatTheDiff (wtd)

> Open-source, deterministic semantic diff CLI. Point it at N files — configs,
> JSON, YAML, Markdown, logs — and it reports what they agree on (consensus),
> how far each file deviates (drift), which files are outliers, and the exact
> evidence (file + line) behind every claim. Written in Zig; zero dependencies;
> static ~300 KB binaries for Linux, macOS, and Windows.

Key facts:
- Answers "what actually matters?" across a whole corpus, not "what changed?" between two files.
- Never compares raw text: files are decomposed into semantic primitives
  (e.g. `db.port=5432`), each hashed with BLAKE3, so key order, whitespace,
  quoting, and comments never register as differences.
- Cross-format canonical unification: the same fact in JSON (including JSONC),
  YAML, INI, XML, or CBOR (RFC 8949 binary) hashes to the same identity, so
  mixed-format corpora find real consensus. XML attributes unify with child
  elements. Lists are index-less (`features[]=x`) — reordering a list is not drift.
- Consensus buckets: universal (in all N files), majority (2k > N),
  minority (1 < k, 2k ≤ N), unique (k = 1). The consensus core is every
  primitive held by a strict majority.
- Drift per file = 1 − Jaccard(its primitives, consensus core); outliers are
  flagged past mean + 1.5σ.
- Factions: groups of files deviating from consensus in the same way, found by
  clustering over minority primitives (Jaccard >= 0.5, union-find). Each
  faction reports its signature — the shared deviations that define it
  (e.g. "region=eu, 3/3 members"). `wtd <dir> --factions` shows just this.
- Conflicts (`wtd <dir> --conflicts`): the odd-one-out report. For each scalar
  key, reports the value the fleet agrees on (plurality) and names exactly which
  files hold a deviant value (e.g. "db.port: 40× 5432, 1× 5433 → prod-17.yaml").
  Cross-format (a JSON value reconciles against the same key in YAML). List keys
  are bags and never flagged; identifier fields where every file differs are
  excluded. Under `--keys-only` no values are compared, so it stays secret-safe.
- Structured executable intelligence: for ELF, PE, and Mach-O binaries, wtd
  extracts the features a reverse engineer triages on — imports, exports,
  section names, needed libraries, and ASCII/UTF-16 strings — as list
  primitives (`imports[]=CreateRemoteThread`, `needs[]=libc.so.6`,
  `sections[]=.text`, `strings[]=...`). They flow through the same
  consensus/drift/faction engine, so a binary that uniquely imports socket or
  TLS functions is the outlier by behavior, with the imports as named evidence
  (not opaque chunk hashes). Parsers validated against nm/readelf/objdump/
  llvm-nm on real binaries; bounds-checked so a hostile binary never crashes.
- Web page / DOM clustering (captured `.html` snapshots): wtd decomposes each
  page into structural facts — a fuzzy tag-stream skeleton (`shape[]` shingles),
  form field names (`field[]`), the form action host (`formaction[]`), and
  external resource hosts (`resource[]`) — and runs the same consensus/drift/
  faction engine. A phishing kit reused across domains clusters into a faction
  even when each deployment is rebranded, with a signature showing what it
  harvests (email/password/otp) and where it posts. Normalization drops hashed
  class names, session tokens, inline styles and injected ads; repeated siblings
  collapse to a bag; reformatting never changes a primitive (property-tested).
  Anchored on phishing-kit / clone detection. `wtd web <url>...` fetches pages
  itself over a zero-dependency std.http client (server-rendered HTML; SPAs need
  an external headless capture fed as .html snapshots); URLs become the artifact
  names and `--snapshot-dir <dir>` saves what was fetched so a run is
  reproducible (fetching is I/O, analysis over the bytes is deterministic).
  Each request has a hard `--timeout <sec>` (default 10) so dead/stalling hosts
  cannot hang the run. For JS-rendered credential forms, capture the rendered
  DOM with a headless browser (e.g. `chromium --headless --dump-dom`) and feed
  the .html to wtd — the zero-dep fetch only sees server-rendered HTML.
  `wtd kit <dir>` emits a kit signature per web family — the harvested form
  fields, form action host, resource hosts, and exclusive skeleton shingles
  (the web analog of `wtd yara`); every atom's witness set equals the member
  set exactly, so it matches the whole family and nothing else in the corpus.
  Text and `wtd.kit.v1` JSON output.
- Candidate YARA rules (`wtd yara <dir>`): turns each detected binary family
  into a detection rule. For every faction it computes the discriminative core —
  features present in every member and absent from every other sample in the
  corpus — and writes a YARA rule from them (imports/strings/sections as text
  atoms, discriminative code chunks as hex). Soundness: an atom is emitted only
  when its witness set equals the family's member set exactly, so it is shared
  across the whole family and matches nothing else in the analyzed corpus
  (property-tested). Deterministic; the anti-yarGen. A candidate to review, not
  a shipped signature, since "absent elsewhere" is only proven vs your corpus.
- CI gate (`wtd <dir> --fail-on <spec>`): exits 3 when the corpus violates a
  policy, so a pipeline can block a bad change. `<spec>` is a comma-separated
  list of conditions — `conflicts[>N]`, `outliers[>N]`, `drift>F` (a bare count
  means "> 0"). Exit codes: 0 ok, 1 error, 2 usage, 3 gate failed (distinct so
  CI can tell a policy violation from a crash). The verdict is also in `--json`
  as a `gate` object (null when the flag is absent). Secret-safe with
  `--keys-only`. Example: `wtd ./configs --fail-on 'conflicts,drift>0.5'`.
- Fully deterministic engine — no AI, no randomness; byte-identical reports.
  Property-tested (seeded oracle suites) and benchmark-verified: ~20 µs/file,
  flat scaling from 1,000 to 1,000,000 files (1M files: 21.8 s, 3.8 GB RSS);
  20,000/20,000 planted outliers detected with zero false positives. The
  streaming evidence store means memory scales with distinct facts, not
  corpus bytes.
- `wtd --json` emits the full evidence graph (schema `wtd.report.v1`) for
  pipelines and AI explanation layers.

Install (checksum-verified):
- Linux/macOS/Git Bash: `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/copyleftdev/whatthediff/main/install.sh | sh`
- Windows PowerShell: `irm https://raw.githubusercontent.com/copyleftdev/whatthediff/main/install.ps1 | iex`

Usage:
- `wtd <dir>` — human report (consensus, drift ranking, outlier evidence)
- `wtd <dir> --drift` / `--consensus` / `--conflicts` — single sections
  (`--conflicts`: the agreed value per key and which files disagree)
- `wtd <dir> --fail-on <spec>` — CI gate; exit 3 on policy violation
  (`conflicts`, `outliers`, `drift>F`, comma-separated)
- `wtd yara <dir>` — emit candidate YARA rules for detected binary families
- `wtd <dir> --json [--evidence]` — machine-readable evidence graph
- `wtd <dir> --keys-only` — compare files by structure, not values (drops the
  value from key=value primitives, hashes raw lines). Secret-safe: point it at
  credential/.env profiles to find schema drift without exposing any value.
  Shell `export KEY=val` is normalized to `KEY`.
- `wtd ask "why is X different?" <dir>` — an LLM explains the deterministic
  evidence with file:line citations; works with the Anthropic API
  (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY), OpenRouter (OPENROUTER_API_KEY), or any local
  OpenAI-compatible endpoint like Ollama (WTD_AI_URL + WTD_AI_MODEL, no key).
  The model only ever sees engine-generated evidence — it cannot invent findings.
  `--dry-run` prints the exact prompt without calling a model.

## Links

- [Source repository](https://github.com/copyleftdev/whatthediff): Zig source, tests, benchmarks
- [README](https://github.com/copyleftdev/whatthediff/blob/main/README.md): full documentation
- [Design philosophy](https://github.com/copyleftdev/whatthediff/blob/main/intent.md): deterministic pipeline, evidence model, AI responsibilities
- [Releases](https://github.com/copyleftdev/whatthediff/releases): static binaries + SHA256SUMS for six platforms
- [Benchmark harness](https://github.com/copyleftdev/whatthediff/blob/main/scripts/bench.sh): reproduce the published numbers
